channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: With Possible Raúl Castro Indictment, U.S. Eyes Venezuela Playbook
item.description: Amid stepped-up surveillance flights, a visit of the C.I.A. director and an energy embargo, the White House is trying to increase pressure on Cuba.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes, Tyler Pager, Eric Schmitt, Michael Crowley and Frances Robles
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Raúl Castro is the former president of Cuba and younger brother of Fidel Castro.

item.title: The Push to Indict Raúl Castro, Cuba’s Former President
item.description: Mr. Castro, Cuba’s former president, could soon face charges in the 1996 killings of four volunteer airmen who were members of a humanitarian group that searched for migrants at sea.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles
item.media:description: Former President Raúl Castro of Cuba attending a May Day parade in Havana this month.

item.title: Photos: Cuba Plunges Into Darkness as U.S. Cuts Off Oil Supply
item.description: The United States has choked off Cuba’s fuel supply, plunging the already impoverished island into an acute energy crisis.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:description: People playing dominoes by flashlight as a protest fire burns in the background in Havana on Thursday.

item.title: Supreme Court Rejects Virginia Democrats’ Effort to Reinstate New Voting Map
item.description: State officials had asked the justices to step in to allow the state to use a congressional map in the midterms that was drawn by Democrats and recently approved by voters.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court’s ruling blocking the map wipes out four newly drawn Democratic-leaning House districts in Virginia.

item.title: Texas Supreme Court Rejects Abbott Effort to Remove Democrats After Walkout
item.description: Republican state leaders tried to expel Democratic lawmakers during their fight over redistricting last year. The court said it was not necessary.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Jim Vondruska for The New York Times
item.media:description: The top Democrat in the Texas House, Gene Wu, was among the lawmakers Gov. Greg Abbott sought to expel last year.

item.title: Trump’s ‘Learning Curve’ on China Ends With Conciliation at Summit
item.description: The president has shifted the foundations of American policy toward China, throwing aside the adversarial approach of recent years.
item.dc:creator: Anton Troianovski and David E. Sanger
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: President Trump and President Xi Jinping of China arriving for the state banquet in Beijing on Thursday.

item.title: Why Were These C.E.O.s in Beijing With Trump?
item.description: Some of America’s most powerful C.E.O.s accompanied President Trump to Beijing during his summit with President Xi Jinping of China. Our reporter Ana Swanson explains what they were hoping to gain from the trip.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson, Nour Idriss, Nikolay Nikolov and James Surdam

item.title: Trump Turns Taiwan Arms Sales Into Bargaining Chip With China
item.description: The president said a potential arms deal for Taiwan was a “very good negotiating chip” in talks with Beijing. His words raise questions about the steadfastness of U.S. support.
item.dc:creator: Chris Buckley
item.media:description: Patriot missile systems on display during a Taiwanese military exercise in Taipei last year.

item.title: For Xi’s Critics, Trump’s China Visit Is Fuel for Jokes They Can’t Tell
item.description: On Threads and other sites, liberal-minded Chinese accounts were mocking the proceedings and offering a rare window into opinions on Xi Jinping and his leadership style.
item.dc:creator: Li Yuan
item.media:description: President Trump was cheered by children upon his arrival in Beijing on Thursday

item.title: Long Island Rail Road Strike Shuts Down Busiest U.S. Passenger Rail Service
item.description: This is the first strike on the service in more than 30 years. It comes after three years of failed contract negotiations, two federal interventions and a volley of last-minute bargaining.
item.dc:creator: Stefanos Chen
item.media:description: The strike is the first on the Long Island Rail Road since 1994.

item.title: Long Island Rail Road Workers Are on Strike. Here’s What to Know.
item.description: America’s busiest passenger rail service shut down on Saturday after unions and transit officials failed to agree on a new contract.
item.dc:creator: Stefanos Chen
item.category: Brotherhood of Locomotive Engineers and Trainmen
International Assn of Machinists and Aerospace Workers
item.media:description: There has not been a strike on the Long Island Rail Road since 1994.

item.title: Claudine Longet, Entertainer Who Shot Olympian Boyfriend, Dies at 84
item.description: A singer and actress, she drew wide attention for the fatal 1976 shooting of Spider Sabich. She was convicted of negligent homicide.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel

item.title: Kataib Hezbollah Commander Accused of Planning Attacks on N.Y.C.
item.description: Mohammad Baqer Saad Dawood al-Saadi is accused of leading attacks in Europe and being part of a retaliation campaign by Iran. Prosecutors say he is a leader of Kataib Hezbollah, an Iraqi militia with ties to Iran.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Weiser, Olivia Bensimon and Maia Coleman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Plot Was ‘Targeting Heart’ of New York’s Jewish Community, Tisch Says
item.description: Police Commissioner Jessica S. Tisch spoke after prosecutors said they had arrested a man planning to attack a Manhattan synagogue.
item.dc:creator: Maria Cramer and Maia Coleman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Commissioner Jessica S. Tisch spoke on Friday at Temple Emanu-El in Manhattan, hours after federal authorities said they disrupted a plot against a synagogue.

item.title: Back From China, Trump Faces Decision on Whether to Resume Strikes on Iran
item.description: Top aides have drafted battle plans as peace negotiations have stalled.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper, Eric Schmitt and Tyler Pager
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: President Trump has not yet decided whether to resume military strikes on Iran, but Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told lawmakers that the Pentagon had “a plan to escalate if necessary.”

item.title: Trump Administration Weighs $1.7 Billion Fund for Allies Investigated Under Biden
item.description: Critics denounced the highly unusual plan, which has yet to be finalized or approved, as a vast political slush fund financed by taxpayers.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush, Andrew Duehren and Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Stefani Reynolds for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump controls both the I.R.S., the agency he sued, and the Justice Department, which would typically represent the I.R.S. against legal claims.

item.title: Snorkeling at Pearl Harbor: Kash Patel’s Travels Add to Focus on Ethical Issues
item.description: The F.B.I. director continues to come under scrutiny for mixing government business with dates, vacation and leisure time.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Williamson and Adam Goldman
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: The memorial above the sunken battleship U.S.S. Arizona in Hawaii in 2016. Kash Patel, the F.B.I. director, was accompanied by Navy SEALs on a “V.I.P. Snorkel” over the site that holds the remains of 1,100 Navy sailors and Marines who died at Pearl Harbor.

item.title: It’s Been a Wild Week in British Politics. What Happens Next?
item.description: Nobody has yet challenged Prime Minister Keir Starmer for the leadership, but his leading rival, Andy Burnham, finally has a route to Downing Street.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:description: Members of the news media congregated outside 10 Downing Street on Thursday, after days of turmoil in the Labour Party over Prime Minister Keir Starmer’s leadership.

item.title: Tina Peters, Colorado Election Denier, Will Be Freed by Gov. Jared Polis
item.description: Gov. Jared Polis, a Democrat, commuted the sentence of Ms. Peters, a former county clerk serving a nine-year sentence for her role in a plot to examine voting machines after the 2020 election.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy and Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Daniel Brenner for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tina Peters, the former clerk of Mesa County, Colo., was sentenced to nine years in prison in 2024 after being found guilty of tampering with voting machines.

item.title: Israeli Strike Targeted Top Hamas Leader in Gaza, Officials Say
item.description: Izz al-Din al-Haddad, the target of an Israeli strike in Gaza City, took over the group’s military wing in Gaza last year. Israeli officials said he was also an architect of the Oct. 7 attack.
item.dc:creator: Alan Yuhas, Jonathan Rosen and Iyad Abuheweila

item.title: Hospital Will Open First ‘Detransition’ Clinic in Legal Settlement With Texas
item.description: Texas Children’s Hospital was under investigation for billing practices on gender-transition treatments. The settlement was expected to end that inquiry.
item.dc:creator: Amy Harmon
item.media:credit: Melissa Phillip/Houston Chronicle, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Texas Children’s Hospital in Houston is one of the largest children’s hospitals in the country.

item.title: Why ‘Smart’ Products Have Started to Look Like the Dumb Choice
item.description: How Wi-Fi-connecting, app-based tech led to a backlash in the name of simplicity.
item.dc:creator: Nitsuh Abebe

item.title: The Damage of ‘Trump Math’ Is Adding Up
item.description: Authoritarians go after data. The president has already started.
item.dc:creator: Binyamin Appelbaum and James Robinson

item.title: Keir Starmer and the End of Britain’s Old Certainties
item.description: The decade since Brexit has been one long, painful process of trying — and failing — to make sense of this new reality.
item.dc:creator: Tom McTague

item.title: OpenAI Bought Company That Offered A.I. Tools for Cloning Voices
item.description: The acquisition, Weights.gg, was a sort of social network for creating and sharing artificial intelligence algorithms.
item.dc:creator: Mike Isaac
item.media:credit: Aaron Wojack for The New York Times
item.media:description: The San Francisco offices of OpenAI, which backed away from earlier efforts to clone voices with artificial intelligence.

item.title: NPR Podcast Host Ramtin Arablouei Exits Amid Workplace Inquiry
item.description: Ramtin Arablouei, a co-host of “Throughline,” left the network after an employee made a human resources complaint about his behavior.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: NPR studios in Washington, D.C.

item.title: Five Italians Die While Scuba Diving Deep Caves in the Maldives
item.description: The divers, including a marine scientist and her daughter, were part of a research trip and were exploring an underwater cave system when they failed to resurface.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel and Josephine de La Bruyère
item.media:description: Divers and guides returning to shore in Malé, capital of the Maldives, a popular diving destination. The disappearance of five divers this week set off a large search mission, but the authorities have recovered only one body.

item.title: How Fights Over West Bank Settlements Are Unfolding at N.Y.C. Synagogues
item.description: Demonstrations outside synagogues have turned real estate sales in Israel and in the occupied territories into a political issue in the city. The protesters’ tactics have disturbed some New Yorkers.
item.dc:creator: Liam Stack
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:credit: Ryan Murphy for The New York Times
item.media:description: Protesters and counterprotesters confronted one another in Midwood, a residential Brooklyn neighborhood with a large Jewish population, earlier this week.
